topics.name: Protein Language Models
topics.blurb: ESM-style embeddings, masked LMs, variant effect, and multimodal target features.

lesson.heading: What a protein LM learns
lesson.body: Protein language models (pLMs) are trained on large sequence corpora with masked or autoregressive objectives. Contextual embeddings capture evolutionary and structural regularities useful for variant effect, binding, and tractability features — not as a replacement for chemistry representations of small molecules.

lesson.heading: Practical CompBio uses
lesson.body: Common patterns: mean-pool residue embeddings for protein-level features; use per-position scores for mutational scanning; combine with structure confidence (pLDDT) and pocket features in multimodal models. Always validate under held-out families/scaffolds.

lesson.heading: Pitfalls
lesson.body: Do not treat embeddings as Tanimoto-like chemical similarity. Watch train/test leakage across homologs. Low-confidence disordered regions can dominate pooled vectors — mask or weight by confidence when appropriate.

code_examples.title: Mean-pool a fake embedding matrix
code_examples.code:
import numpy as np

def mean_pool(residue_emb: np.ndarray, mask: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """residue_emb: [L, D], mask: [L] bool — return [D]."""
    w = mask.astype(float)[:, None]
    return (residue_emb * w).sum(0) / max(mask.sum(), 1)

emb = np.random.randn(128, 64)
mask = np.ones(128, dtype=bool)
mask[100:] = False
protein_vec = mean_pool(emb, mask)
print(protein_vec.shape)
code_examples.explain: Masked mean-pooling turns variable-length residue embeddings into a fixed protein vector for downstream classifiers.

topics.quiz_focus: protein language models, ESM embeddings, variant effect prediction, multimodal target features, homology leakage

topics.name: Protein Folding
topics.blurb: AlphaFold/OpenFold confidence, construct design, and using predicted structures in discovery.

lesson.heading: Confidence is the product
lesson.body: Predicted structures are only as trustworthy as their confidence metrics. pLDDT flags local reliability; PAE describes relative domain orientations. Docking or pocket detection on low-pLDDT loops is a common failure mode.

lesson.heading: From sequence to assay construct
lesson.body: Fold models help propose domain boundaries with disorder predictions. Combine Pfam/UniProt domains with pLDDT drop-offs to design soluble expression constructs for kinases and other targets.

lesson.heading: Holo vs apo reality
lesson.body: AF models are typically apo-like. Ligand-induced fit, cryptic pockets, and cofactors may be missing. Ensemble or holo templates still matter for structure-based design.

code_examples.title: Filter residues by pLDDT
code_examples.code:
def confident_residues(plddt, threshold=70):
    return [i for i, s in enumerate(plddt) if s >= threshold]

plddt = [90, 88, 55, 40, 92]
print(confident_residues(plddt))
code_examples.explain: Simple confidence gating before using coordinates for pocket or interface analysis.

topics.quiz_focus: AlphaFold pLDDT PAE, construct design, apo vs holo limitations, using predicted structures for docking

topics.name: Docking Studies
topics.blurb: Grid boxes, protomers, pose clustering, IFPs, enrichment, and covalent protocols.

lesson.heading: Prep beats scoring
lesson.body: Protonation/tautomers, pocket definition, and waters/cofactors often matter more than which engine you pick. Wrong protomers silently destroy H-bond networks.

lesson.heading: After the dock
lesson.body: Cluster poses, compute interaction fingerprints, rescore, and filter strain/clashes. Early enrichment metrics communicate VS quality — with decoy bias caveats.

lesson.heading: Flexibility & covalents
lesson.body: Loop-gated pockets need ensembles or induced fit. Covalent libraries need warhead-aware constraints to the nucleophilic residue.

code_examples.title: RMSD-style pose cluster seed
code_examples.code:
import numpy as np

def greedy_cluster(rmsd: np.ndarray, cutoff=2.0):
    """rmsd[i,j] pairwise; return representative indices."""
    n = rmsd.shape[0]
    unused = set(range(n))
    reps = []
    while unused:
        i = min(unused)
        reps.append(i)
        unused -= {j for j in unused if rmsd[i, j] <= cutoff}
    return reps

R = np.array([[0, 1.1, 3.5], [1.1, 0, 3.2], [3.5, 3.2, 0]])
print(greedy_cluster(R))
code_examples.explain: Greedy clustering compresses redundant docking poses into diverse representatives.

topics.quiz_focus: docking prep, pose clustering, interaction fingerprints, enrichment metrics, covalent docking, induced fit

topics.name: Python Code Reading
topics.blurb: What does this code do? BioPython, RDKit, pandas, and NumPy snippets from discovery workflows.

lesson.heading: Read for side effects
lesson.body: Interview code questions test whether you see I/O formats, None returns (MolFromSmiles), unit coercion, and group-aware splits — not trivia about syntax sugar.

lesson.heading: CompBio idioms
lesson.body: SeqIO.parse vs read, sanitize after SMILES parse, pd.to_numeric(errors='coerce'), and scaffold-grouped CV are recurring patterns.

lesson.heading: How to answer
lesson.body: State inputs/outputs, name the failure modes (invalid SMILES, mixed units, leakage), and suggest the one-line fix.

code_examples.title: Spot the leakage
code_examples.code:
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'compound_id': ['A','A','B','B'],
    'dose': [1, 10, 1, 10],
    'pIC50': [5.1, 5.1, 6.2, 6.2],
})
# BUG: splits wells, not compounds
train, test = train_test_split(df, test_size=0.5, random_state=0)
print(train['compound_id'].tolist(), test['compound_id'].tolist())
code_examples.explain: Random row splits leak the same compound into train and test. Split on compound_id/scaffold groups instead.

topics.quiz_focus: reading BioPython RDKit pandas code, identifying bugs, SeqIO parse vs read, MolFromSmiles None, scaffold split leakage

topics.name: BiTE & T-cell Engagers
topics.blurb: Bispecific T-cell engagers: formats, CD3 engagement, developability, and cyno cross-reactivity.

lesson.heading: Mechanism
lesson.body: BiTEs (bispecific T-cell engagers) bind a tumor-associated antigen and CD3 to redirect T-cell cytotoxicity. Geometry, affinity, and valency tune synapse formation and cytokine risk.

lesson.heading: Formats & half-life
lesson.body: Classic scFv-scFv BiTEs are potent but short-lived; Fc-containing engagers extend half-life. CompBio supports developability, immunogenicity, and ortholog epitope checks for tox species.

lesson.heading: Safety & biomarkers
lesson.body: CRS risk, on-target/off-tumor antigen expression, and epitope conservation across human/cyno are central translational questions.

code_examples.title: Flag antigen expression risk
code_examples.explain: Simple expression triage for on-target/off-tumor risk before engager nomination.

topics.quiz_focus: BiTE mechanism, CD3 engagement, formats half-life, cytokine release, cyno cross-reactivity, off-tumor antigen risk

topics.name: Antibody-Drug Conjugates
topics.blurb: ADC anatomy: mAb, linker, payload; DAR; internalization; bystander effect.

lesson.heading: Three-part system
lesson.body: ADCs combine a targeting antibody, cleavable or non-cleavable linker, and cytotoxic payload. Efficacy depends on antigen selectivity, internalization, and linker stability in circulation.

lesson.heading: DAR & heterogeneity
lesson.body: Drug-to-antibody ratio (DAR) affects potency, clearance, and aggregation. Site-specific conjugation reduces heterogeneity versus stochastic lysine coupling.

lesson.heading: Payload logic
lesson.body: Membrane-permeable payloads can show bystander killing in heterogeneous tumors; impermeable payloads spare neighbors. CompBio joins internalization assays, antigen density, and PK.

code_examples.title: Estimate average DAR from species mix
code_examples.explain: Weighted average DAR from a deconvoluted conjugate distribution.

topics.quiz_focus: ADC antibody linker payload, DAR, cleavable vs non-cleavable linkers, bystander effect, site-specific conjugation

topics.name: QSAR & Molecular ML
topics.blurb: Descriptors, fingerprints, scaffold splits, calibration, and active learning for potency models.

lesson.heading: Labels first
lesson.body: Unit harmonization, qualified values, and parent-compound dedup beat fancy architectures on messy ChEMBL-like tables.

lesson.heading: Split like discovery
lesson.body: Scaffold/time splits expose generalization; random splits leak analogues and inflate AUROC.

lesson.heading: Decide with uncertainty
lesson.body: Ensembles and conformal methods support medchem intervals and active-learning acquisition under assay budgets.

code_examples.title: pIC50 conversion
code_examples.explain: 100 nM → pIC50 7.0 using molar units inside the log.

topics.quiz_focus: QSAR descriptors fingerprints, ChEMBL curation, scaffold splits, calibration, active learning

topics.name: CRISPR & Genomics Ops
topics.blurb: Guide design, GC, off-targets, reference builds, and expression joins.

lesson.heading: Guides are genomic objects
lesson.body: On-target scores, GC, and genome-wide off-target aligners dominate — not chemistry filters meant for small molecules.

lesson.heading: Pin the build
lesson.body: GRCh37 vs GRCh38 coordinate mismatches silently break peak/variant joins. Pin and document the assembly.

code_examples.title: GC fraction
code_examples.explain: Basic GC helper analogous to Bio.SeqUtils.gc_fraction for oligo windows.

topics.quiz_focus: CRISPR oligo GC, off-target search, reference genome builds, RNA-seq ID mapping
